3A_Bibliographic.citation: Hisler, G. C., Hasler, B. P., Franzen, P. L., Clark, D. B., & Twenge, J. M. (2020). Screen media use and sleep disturbance symptom severity in children. Sleep Health, 6(6), 731-742.

3B_Sample.N: ~10,666-10,676 (varies by analysis due to listwise deletion; from 11,878 in ABCD Release 2.0)

3C_Exposure.exposure_measure: ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey - 6 screen media types for weekday and weekend
3C_Exposure.how_operationalized: Dichotomized: >=2 hours vs <2 hours for each modality on weekdays and weekends separately, based on pediatric recommendations. Also continuous non-TV and total screen time variables. Weekday and weekend assessed separately.

3D_Outcome.measure_used: Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children (SDSC) - parent report. 26 items on 1-5 Likert scale. Subscales: insomnia, sleep disordered breathing, arousal disturbances, sleep-wake transition disturbances, excessive sleepiness, sleep hyperhidrosis. Also: sleep duration (single parent-report item), sleep onset latency (single item), total sleep-wake disturbance score (threshold >=39 for clinical significance). Insufficient sleep dichotomized per NSF guidelines.
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: Sleep duration mean: 9.00 hrs (SD=1.11); Sleep onset latency mean: 1.93 (SD=0.99); Total sleep-wake disturbance mean: 36.54 (SD=8.24)

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Estimated marginal means comparing >=2 hrs vs <2 hrs of each screen type on each sleep outcome, adjusted for covariates. Cohen's d effect sizes reported. Logistic regressions for insufficient sleep (relative risk) and disturbed sleep (relative risk). Continuous screen time as predictor with odds ratios per hour increase. Ethnoracial moderation tested via interaction terms. Bonferroni-corrected threshold p<.001.
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, parent employment, family income, parent education, race (White=0, non-White=1), ethnicity (Hispanic/Latinx), gender
3E_Design_Analysis.SES_in_model: yes - family income and parent education were covariates
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: To examine the association between screen media use and sleep, mean levels of sleep variables were compared between children with more than 2 hours of screen use vs. those with less for the different types of screen medias.

3F_Effect_Sizes.CI_95: not reported for individual d values
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Average absolute d across all screen types and all sleep variables. Range of d for specific modalities: TV avg d=0.07, video avg d=0.11, video game avg d=0.09-0.10, texting avg d=0.09, social media avg d=0.12-0.14, video chat avg d=0.09-0.12, non-TV screen use avg d=0.10, total screen use avg d=0.08-0.10
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Three important patterns emerge from these effect sizes. First, the effect sizes were small, but varied in magnitude (range = 0.07-0.14).
3F_Effect_Sizes.below_threshold: yes (all d values are well below 0.20 threshold)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Sleep duration: >=2hrs vs <2hrs screen time. Weekday TV d=-0.14, weekend TV d=-0.07, weekday video d=-0.14, weekend video d=-0.27, weekday game d=-0.19, weekend game d=-0.14, social media weekday d=-0.28, social media weekend d=-0.14, non-TV weekday d=-0.22, total weekday d=-0.21
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Except for video chat and texting use on weekends and TV use on weekdays, children with two or more hours of screen use of any type of screen media on either weekdays or weekends had shorter sleep durations (average d = -0.19, all Ps < .001)
3F_Effect_Sizes.below_threshold: yes (most d values below 0.20; social media weekday d=-0.28 exceeds but based on only 2% of sample)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Relative risk of insufficient sleep for >=2 hrs vs <2 hrs of TV, video, video game use. Range across weekday/weekend comparisons.
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: children who engaged in two or more hours of TV, video, and video game use on the weekday or weekend had a greater risk of having insufficient sleep (relative risk range 1.23 to 1.60, all Ps < .001)
3F_Effect_Sizes.below_threshold: no (RR 1.23-1.60 are outside 0.80-1.20 for the higher values)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Per one hour increase in non-TV/total screen media -> odds of insufficient sleep
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: each one hour increase in screen media use was associated with an increase in the odds of having insufficient sleep (odds ratios range: 1.08-1.10, all Ps < .001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Per one hour increase in non-TV/total screen media -> odds of disturbed sleep
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: each one hour increase in both non-TV screen media and total screen media use predicted increased odds of having insufficient or disturbed sleep

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Weekend social media >=2hrs vs <2hrs -> total sleep-wake disturbance score (largest single d in Tables 2-3 for a disturbance subscale comparison)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: two or more hours of social media use on weekend was associated with greater overall sleep-wake disturbance symptom severity (d = 0.22, P< .001)
3F_Effect_Sizes.below_threshold: no (d=0.22 marginally exceeds 0.20 threshold, but based on only 2% of sample)

3G_Interpretation.CIs_reported: no (individual CIs for effect sizes not reported)
3G_Interpretation.large_N_acknowledged: yes - authors explicitly note effect sizes are small (d~0.20) and discuss this
3G_Interpretation.verbatim_recommendation: these findings highlight that regulating screen use may be one way to improve sleep in childhood and adolescence. Such regulations could be implemented individually with the child as well as within the family as parents play a protective regulatory role in screen media use as well as provide role models for its use.

3H_Rhetorical.pattern_detail: Authors explicitly acknowledge d~0.20 effects are 'frequently interpreted as small' but then argue they are important because 'sleep is a daily behavior that carries profound economic, mental and physical health consequences' and children are 'a population in which sleep is particularly vulnerable.' This is a nuanced acknowledge-then-contextualize pattern. Recommendations still issued despite small effects.
3H_Rhetorical.list_1_qualifier: yes - 'It is important to note that these studies tended to report effect sizes of approximately d = 0.20. Such effects sizes are frequently interpreted as small'
3H_Rhetorical.list_2_qualifier: yes - paper notes cross-sectional limitation and bidirectional possibilities

3I_Flags.ambiguity_notes: Preregistered study. Effect sizes explicitly reported and acknowledged as small. Very few children (2-4%) used social media or video chat >=2 hrs, limiting power for those modalities. Cross-sectional design. Ethnoracial moderation interactions all non-significant at p<.001. Social media had largest average d but smallest cell sizes. Authors note the per-hour ORs of 1.05-1.10 translate to very small absolute risk changes.
